Starting Treatment with Children and Adolescents: A Process-Oriented Guide for Therapists. By Steven Tuber and Jane Caflisch. New York: Routledge, 2011, xvi + 308 pp., $42.95 paperback.

Anne Adelman

Anne Adelman

5480 Wisconsin Avenue, Suite 215-A
Chevy Chase, MD 20815
E-mail: adelmanaj@comcast.net

Anne Adelman   Member, Contemporary Freudian Society; faculty, New Directions Program, Washington Institute for Psychoanalysis.

In Starting Treatment with Children and Adolescents, Steven Tuber and Jane Caflisch bring us into the wild and sometimes chaotic world of play

191

therapy, with all its unexpected delights and mysteries. While the book is geared to the beginning therapist, all clinicians working with children will find themselves challenged and engaged by it. Unlike other introductory works, this volume is unique in that it uses raw material from beginning therapists—material as difficult, forthright, and honest as any that therapists will likely encounter early in their careers, or indeed, throughout their professional lives.

Using a close process technique, the authors, drawing on Tuber's experience and wisdom and Caflisch's fresh eye and clinical astuteness, intersperse carefully transcribed process material with supervisory-style reflections on these vignettes. Thus, in an accessible and engaging manner the authors provide us alternating views into the minds of the clinician, the supervisor, and the child patient. Reading this book is like participating in a child therapy seminar of the highest order, one that allows for frank discussion of what has actually transpired in the playroom between child patient and therapist—omitting neither the good nor the bad nor, at times, the ugly. In this vein, they offer an indispensable lesson in child therapy, specifically, that there will always be a second chance, a time to revisit and rework clinical challenges.

The book has at its heart several main themes that are interwoven throughout. The first, embracing all the others, is that therapy is a self-reflective process whereby the therapist invites the child patient to join in and wonder alongside one another. This is an essential concept for the child therapist. By adapting this stance, the therapist encourages the child to become curious about his or her internal experience and to develop the sort of psychological mindedness that permits introspection. Over time, the authors suggest, this stance allows the child to begin to regulate intense emotions, to develop greater problem-solving strategies, and ultimately to experience more positive interactions in the world. “Verbalization,” they write, “… allows for the potential transformation of … experience” (p. 2).

[[RunningHead]]

A Dream of Freedom: The Correspondence of Sigmund Freud and Nikolay Y. Ossipov 1921-1929

Galina Hristeva

Galina Hristeva

Keplerstrasse 17
Stuttgart 70174
GERMANY
E-mail: galina.hristeva@ilw.uni-stuttgart.de

Galina Hristeva  

The correspondence of Sigmund Freud and Nikolay Y. Ossipov, a Russian psychoanalyst and emigré from the Bolshevik terror, was published for the first time in Germany in 2009. It reveals various ways in which psychoanalysis was first disseminated in Eastern Europe and sheds light on Ossipov's contribution to psychoanalysis, especially his concept of the ego's “cooperative complexity.” Along with viewing the correspondence as a tool capable of liberating creativity and stimulating scientific production—a perspective that may open up a new and promising research field—special focus is placed on Freud's response to Ossipov's efforts to expand psychoanalysis and link it with literature and speculative philosophy. A leitmotif of the letters is the freedom of science and the different reactions of the two men to the threats posed by politics. Freud's warm and compassionate response to the precarious situation and creative efforts of Ossipov, the first analyst in exile, is examined.

Comings and Goings

Steven Levy

Steven Levy

Steven Levy  

It is a bittersweet moment as I pen my last “Comings and Goings” in my role as editor of JAPA. Bonnie Litowitz assumes the editorship of JAPA as of January 2014. Bonnie will work closely with me as editor-elect during 2013. She is intimately familiar with the pleasures and perils of editing JAPA, having served as my associate editor for many years, as well as serving in a similar capacity during the editorship of Arnold Richards, my predecessor. Bonnie brings enormous erudition and enthusiasm to our journal.

Jeanine M. Vivona has received the 2012 JAPA Prize for her article “Is There a Nonverbal Period of Development?” which appeared in JAPA 60/2.

Diane E. Donnelly is the inaugural winner of the JAPA New Author Prize for her article “The Function of Suffering as Portrayed in The Scarlet Letter and Reflected in Clinical Work,” which was published in JAPA 60/6. I continue to encourage new authors to submit their work to JAPA. The New Author Prize was created to promote this effort. Details about the prize appear in this issue, and I hope readers will bring it to the attention of their colleagues.

JAPA podcasts—in-depth interviews with authors of papers recently published in the journal—explore these authors' scholarly and clinical interests, as well as possible extensions of their ideas into adjacent disciplines. In addition to podcasts with Man Ruti and Jeanine Vivona, we have recently added an interview with Lewis Kirshner about his article “Toward an Ethics of Psychoanalysis: A Critical Reading of Lacan's Ethics,” which appeared in JAPA 60/6.

Thanks to those who gave so generously of their time as Editorial Board members from January 1, 2010, through December 31, 2012: Stephen B. Bernstein, Daria Colombo, Anne Erreich, James P. Frosch, James F. Leckman, Janice S. Lieberman, Ellen Pinsky, Donnel B. Stern, and Jeanine M. Vivona. Each of these board members has made important contributions on behalf of JAPA. I look forward to further collaboration with them in the future.

Few generations, if any, have lived through as many traumatic changes in political systems, officially prevailing values, and social structures as has the generation of Germans born during the first quarter of the twentieth century. The tight class structure of the monarchical Wilhelminian Empire gave way, in 1918-1919, to the turmoil and democratic promise of the Weimar Republic (1918-1933) only to be swept aside by the totalitarian Third Reich (1933-1945) with its military expansionism, racist policies, and inhumane destructiveness, to be followed by total defeat, destruction, occupation, and a complete revaluation of values issuing eventually in two German states. Both of them were founded in 1947, based on starkly different principles and values resulting in the very separate societies and polities known as the Federal Republic of Germany, or FRG, often referred to as the Bonn Republic, and the German Democratic Republic, or GDR, which in 1990 officially merged into the FRG.

Mari Ruti is an associate professor of critical theory at the University of Toronto with a long-standing interest in psychoanalysis, having contributed to this journal and participated in annual meetings of the American

1052

Psychoanalytic Association. She is the author of several scholarly books and papers, mostly related to her attempt to sort out the implications of post-Lacanian studies in contemporary critical theory. Her paper “The Singularity of Being: Lacan and the Immortal Within” (2010), the basis of the book under review, was awarded the JAPA prize.

Ruti's work reflects the verve and intellectual passion of the authors she engages: the pioneering feminist philosopher Judith Butler; the omnipresent Slovenian Marxist-Lacanian Slovaj Žižek; the theologian-ethicist Eric Santner; the Parisian philosopher and playwright Alain Badiou; the radical queer theorist Lee Edelman; the Slovenian philosopher Alenka Zupančič; and many others. This otherwise diverse group share an interest in building upon the conceptual innovations of Jacques Lacan, the key work being Lacan's seminar The Ethics of Psychoanalysis. In The Singularity of Being: Lacan and the Immortal Within, Ruti presents her own, more humanistic version of ethics in what has been an ongoing dialogue with the post-Lacanians. She has written a dense and intense work, illuminating a range of difficult ideas and raising a challenge to radical strains of ethical theory that she considers dead ends.

The problem can be summarized briefly. Human beings are biological creatures integrated from birth into a hegemonic symbolic system of rules and power structures that determine the course of their individual lives. To define themselves, they must have recourse to the terms given them, which implicitly validate the assumptions of the systems that perpetuate them, what she refers to as an “irremediable disempowerment” (p. 108) that “colonizes” the living being (p. 113). At their core, however, human subjects carry a piece of what is unassimilable to symbolic representation, a residue of the real that resists all signification and personalization—“a defiant part of us that has not been entirely broken” (p. 125). The Lacanian “real” is at once the source of what makes us unique, singular persons and an alienating, even demonic, element that cannot be mastered or understood. It is what saves us from inescapable subjection to forces of oppression or to a Kafkesque Other, while disrupting all attempts to harmonize or humanize raw existence. “Perhaps the purest expression of this inhuman element” in ordinary life, Ruti writes, “is the meaningless facial tic, spasm, or cringe” that Lacan called “the grimace of the real” (p. 41). In this example, the real presents itself as an unsettling intrusion into the illusions of intersubjective relations, confronting us with a radical otherness with which we cannot identify.

L. Keller

5435 College Avenue, #201
Oakland, CA 94618
E-mail: dr.keller@kellerphd.com

L. Keller   Faculty, San Francisco Center for Psychoanalysis.

Many years ago, perusing the stacks of the Erik Erikson Library in the San Francisco Center for Psychoanalysis, I came upon a small publication, a little monograph from the New York Review of Books called Teaching the “Unteachable” (Kohl 1967). It is the story of a young man, teaching in an impoverished area of New York City, who threw out the book and began to listen to his students, to ask them to write their own thoughts, about their own lives, and to read what they wrote. Herbert Kohl is not a psychoanalyst, rather, an educator, but he happened on one of the principles on which clinical psychoanalysis is founded: we all need to be seen and reflected in the eyes and mind of the other in order to know ourselves (Winnicott 1971; Fonagy and Target 1996). I thought often of Herbert Kohl's small book of children's writings as I read Michael O'Loughlin's rich and complex collection of work from a host of visionary practitioners of applied psychoanalysis, who each in their own way use their psychoanalytic training and thinking to better the lives of children, from the Outback of Australia to the U.K., France, and the U.S.

This collection ranges from clinicians whose work with parents draws on all of their clinical and theoretical resources (Donna Wolf-Palacio, Carola Chase) to program builders (Leon Hoffman et al., Norma Tracey) who, like the composer of a symphony, hold all of the players in mind (administrators, educators, parents, and most of all children) as they work to make their vision of the pragmatic yet sophisticated psychoanalytic work that is possible with children in the bricks-and-mortar world. I found it helpful in my reading to think of the contributions as loosely organized around two areas: clinical work, recognizable to any psychoanalyst practicing today, and work on the theory and practice of education.

Religion remains one of the most powerful forces motivating human beings, and indeed entire societies and nations. Its influence is in many

1259

ways transformed, but is still unbroken, despite advances in science and insight. Its study has fascinated scholars of many stripes, and psychoanalysis has continually tried to deal with its many manifestations and varied history; developments in religious studies have always evoked a profound interest among analysts. The last few years have opened up new insights into the development of Western religion. I will mention but two German contributions that could profoundly influence our understanding of Judaism and Christianity. One pertains to the introduction of monotheism in human history, a topic that moved Freud to write his last book, Moses and Monotheism, and is the subject of two important works by the Egyptologist Jan Assmann: the religion of ancient Egypt showed in its later development a strong tendency to merge all the gods into the one God Amun, the “Hidden One,” and thus to become monotheistic, even before the great Pharaoh Echnaton established during his brief reign a rigorous monotheistic system focused on Aton, the sun god (c. 1350 B.C.). A thousand years later something very similar happened in Greek and Roman antiquity: religion became imbued with a belief in “the One and All—to Hen kai Pan.” And yet, there was a dramatic difference between such an inclusive monotheism, which encompassed all the pagan divinities within its one God, and the Biblical deity: the latter's exclusivity.

Assmann (2003) starts out with the “psychohistorical aspect,” stressed above all by Freud: “The turn to monotheism with its ethical demands, its emphasis on the inner man and its character as ‘father religion’ is joined with a new mental attitude and new ‘nature of soul,’ that have decisively shaped the Western view of mankind” (p. 12). He adds: “I call the crucial point of this turn ‘The Mosaic Distinction.’ It is not the distinction between the One God and the many gods that seems to me decisive, but the distinction between true and false in religion, between the true God and the false gods, the true teaching and the erroneous teachings, between knowledge and ignorance, between faith and wrong belief” (pp. 12-13). Specifically, this development “coincides with the ‘invention of inner man.’ The distinction between truth and lie does not only divide the outer space, but it cuts midway through the human heart which now truly becomes the arena of religious dynamics” (p. 156). The discovery of the inner person is also the discovery of inner conflict as something deeply constitutive of mental life. Dynamics means inner conflict. This distinction, however, is intimately conjoined with what Assmann (2006) calls “the language of violence.

Hope for the future … knowledge of the operation of the hormones.

—Freud (1933)

It did seem to give a little bit more permission to feel sexy, attractive when you're pregnant.

—Demi Moore

In their foreword to Rosemary Balsam's Women's Bodies in Psychoanalysis, Nancy Chodorow and Adrienne Harris write that “since Freud told us that anatomy is destiny, and that anatomy was, explicitly, the presence or absence of the penis, it has been hard for analysts to conceptualize gender and sexuality apart from this particular sexual difference. Correspondingly, many contributors to the psychoanalytic literature on women have been leery of making women's bodies, or any anatomical destiny, front and central” (p. xii).

In this book Balsam reintroduces women's bodies into psychoanalytic thinking, but in a manner more nuanced and scientific than we are accustomed to seeing. Balsam's groundbreaking work can allow those of us who engage with the study of gender and sexuality to expand on her labor (pun consciously not intended) and to integrate psychoanalytic ideas with contemporary findings from neuropsychology, endocrinology, and other disciplines. Balsam's work is in the tradition of Freud at his scientific best.

Balsam's volume consists of eleven chapters plus an introduction. Much of the material in most chapters has been presented and/or published elsewhere in various versions.

George Hagman   Director of Clinical Services, Greater Bridgeport Community Mental Health Center; faculty, National Psychological Association for Psychoanalysis; faculty, Westchester Center for the Study of Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy.

Over the past fifteen years Allan Schore's several books, numerous articles, and many talks have had an important impact on the world of psychoanalysis and analytic psychotherapy. (Schore is on the clinical faculty, University of California David Geffen School of Medicine.) For a profession hungry for validation and overwhelmed by the difficulty,

196

inaccessibility, and sheer volume of research findings, Schore, through his literature reviews and integration of interdisciplinary research, has been a lifesaver and a cheerleader for psychoanalysis. His emphasis on the importance of affective communication embedded in unconscious right brain functioning has provided research-based neuropsychiatric support for recent clinical and theoretical advances in the areas of trauma, attachment, and developmental pathologies. Reading Schore is both an experience of the new, evident especially in his ability to describe research that might be unknown to clinicians, and of the old, as if he were saying something we already know and practice. Affective resonance and responsiveness, the importance of empathy, the function of dissociation and projection, the crucial (perhaps central) role of nonverbal and embodied communication, all have been discussed at length in the recent analytic literature. Even before Schore, not many would have disputed their importance. But it is nice to be told that research has verified that we are on the right path and that the therapeutic approaches we have developed have “proven effectiveness.”

Schore's new volume, The Science of the Art of Psychotherapy, continues his ongoing effort to report on and integrate the vast literature that has accumulated over the past several decades. The book is divided into two parts, the first five chapters addressing issues of affect regulation, clinical neuropsychoanalysis, and psychotherapy, and the final six dealing with a range of issues including family law, ethology, and the etiology of borderline personality disorder. I will focus here mostly on the first part, which deals more directly with clinical matters of greater interest to psychoanalytic practitioners.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Teaching Psychoanalytic Concepts, Skills, and Attitudes to Medical Students

David Mintz

David Mintz

25 Main Street, P.O. Box 962
Stockbridge, MA 01262
E-mail: david.mintz@austenriggs.net

David Mintz  

In recent decades the psychoanalytic perspective has been increasingly marginalized in medical school education. This has been driven by political tides, competition with neuroscience education, shifting practice models, and the dominance of an evidence-based perspective. The costs both to medical trainees and to the field of psychoanalysis are considerable. Reasons are presented for why academic psychiatry departments might be motivated to integrate more psychodynamic training into basic medical education and for why organized psychoanalysis might have an interest in developing a greater presence in medical schools. Various formats are discussed in which psychoanalytic theory and technique might effectively be introduced. Finally, broader strategies are proposed for addressing students and their developmental needs such that resistances to learning psychodynamics are reduced.

Joan Wexler

Joan Wexler

255 Bradley Street
New Haven, CT 06510
E-mail: joan.wexler@yale.edu

Joan Wexler   Faculty, Western New England Institute for Psychoanalysis; faculty, Yale University Division of Mental Hygiene.

Collections of poems written by psychoanalysts raise questions: what are the similarities and differences, for example, between poetry and psychoanalysis, and what distinguishes a poem written by a psychoanalyst from poems written by others?

The shared experience of the analyst and analysand is a creation unique to the particular characteristics of each person and to the present changing moment in their personal histories. The joining of a poem and a reader is also a unique mix; although the poem remains constant, the reader is not and the poet is absent. Analyst and analysand are both present, though not necessarily constant. Analyst, analysand, and reader share the experience of subjectively engaging with the voice of another. Poetry is a created product, analysis a created process.

This beautiful and memorable work on the transmission of trauma across generations bears witness to the unfolding effects of trauma, at once devastating and often pervasively subtle and difficult to recognize. Its value lies in its many illustrations of the ways in which recognition of multigenerational trauma is directly helpful in liberating our patients from their suffering. Originating in two conferences held at the Erikson Institute at the Austen Riggs Center, its contributions are unusually coherent for an edited work, as well as consistent in their high quality. M. Gerard Fromm's editorship and introduction to the book, and his two integrative essays, deserve much of the credit for this.

Anne Hoffman   Professor of English and Comparative Literature, Fordham University; research affiliation, DeWitt Wallace Institute for the History of Psychiatry, Weill Cornell Medical College and Columbia Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

Robert Pinsky (2008) notes that psychoanalysis and poetry share the understanding “that forgetting and remembering are interdependent parts of one process” (p. 1082), an observation that might stand as preface to Uta Gosmann's study of poetic memory in the work of Sylvia Plath, Susan Howe, Ellen Hinsey, and Louise Glück. In this study of four contemporary poets, Gosmann proposes the concept of poetic memory as a way of formulating an understanding of the poetic self or subject as permeable, open to the past in ways that go beyond the boundaries of an individual life or the documentary record of events.

How is a poem like an analysis? Each is an intersubjective encounter that occurs within a defined space. In the space of that encounter, whether it is the analytic session or the poem, the past makes itself heard in the voices of the present. In poetry, as in analytic work, language is not only the medium of expression, but the object of study, the focus of interest and awareness. For analyst and analysand, writer and reader, language is dense with meaning, in ways that no individual can ever control or exhaust.

A closer look at the kinship of poetry and psychoanalysis takes us into the realm of metaphor. Borbely (1998) develops a psychoanalytic conception of metaphor as an operation of mind that indicates freedom of movement, freedom to explore unexpected connections or juxtapostions, in contrast to traumatic experience that “leads to a degradation of metaphorical processes” (p. 924). Similarly, Shaddock (2010)

Clinical Themes

The Clinical Problem of Masochism. Edited by Deanna Holtzman and Nancy Kulish. Lanham, MD: Jason Aronson, 2012, ix + 209 pp., $65.00.

Harry Trosman

Harry Trosman

5841 South Maryland Avenue
Chicago, IL 60637
E-mail: htrosman@yoda.bsd.uchicago.edu

Harry Trosman   Professor of Psychiatry and Behavioral Neuroscience, University of Chicago; Training and Supervising Analyst, Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis.

This book grew out of a series of American Psychoanalytic Association discussion groups devoted to clinical issues of masochism. The topic is highly germane, since masochism as a clinical entity has moved from being regarded as a symptom, a type of sexual perversion in the limited sense, to take on a more encompassing sense. Having attained an expanded scope in psychoanalytic thinking, the concept has allowed analysts to view the clinical picture in a variety of ways. Many see the search, tolerance, and desire for pain, suffering, and humiliation, as experienced by many patients, as expressions of masochism. The book calls to mind a similar collection, edited a quarter of a century ago by Robert Glick and Donald Meyers, Masochism: Clinical Psychoanalytic Perspectives. Two of the contributors to that volume, Glick and Stanley Coen, are contributors to this volume as well.

Before giving an overview of our current thinking on masochism, I will summarize each contribution. The volume is led off by Otto Kernberg, who emphasizes an object relations framework and points out the importance of general personality structure, particularly narcissistic and borderline types, to theory and technique. Victims subject to sexual abuse in childhood and traumatized object relations will vary in their ways of handling object relations and may need to subject themselves to personal pain and humiliation; some will long for narcissistic partners to control through their suffering. Therapeutic considerations will frequently involve the transferential reliving of sadomasochistic repetitions in which the analyst is persecuted as the object of attack. Particularly in work with borderline patients, Kernberg has found transference-focused psychotherapy effective; he warns against countertransference emotion, often a response to the patient's persecutory aggressive impulse.

Howard Levine

124 Dean Road
Brookline, MA 02445

Howard Levine   Faculty, Psychoanalytic Institute of New England, East; faculty and Supervising Analyst, Massachusetts Institute for Psychoanalysis.

Toward the end of his life, after more than fifty years of psychoanalytic practice, André Green turned his attention to what he termed his “least fortunate experiences” (p. xxvii): that is, patients he had attempted to treat or whose treatments he had consulted on and who did not fare well. Like Freud (1937) in “Analysis Terminable and Interminable,” Green was looking back across a lifetime of clinical experience, and while he was mindful of the many patients he had helped and had come to understand, he asked himself what if anything he could in retrospect learn from considering his least successful cases.

But where Freud proposed that innate, constitutional factors—“resistances from the Id”—determined the limits of what was therapeutically possible, Green hypothesized that what might look like constitutional factors because they develop so early, and are so long-lasting and resistant to change, are more probably a set of disturbances resulting from very early, preverbal traumata.1 For Green, this last term included not only the initial event—e.g., a needed object who is hurtful or seriously neglectful, or who fails to soothe or relieve the infant's distress; the sudden loss of emotional availability of a once loving mother as in the Dead Mother Syndrome (Green 1980)—but also the unfortunate ways in which that trauma and its consequences were reacted to by the external environment, especially the ongoing failures of the external world to mitigate and relieve those consequences. In Green's view, this combination of factors produces ego deformations that disrupt the creation of a psychic framing structure, an internal space in which emerging experience can be psychically represented. In the absence of that structure, the trajectory of subsequent psychic

1“Today, we would not be satisfied with invoking an innate libidinal … [etiology for these difficulties]; rather, they would be seen as a product of early experiences, fixed by the vicissitudes of development” (p. 65).

Robert Stolorow

2444 Wilshire Boulevard, #624

Robert Stolorow   Training and Supervising Analyst, Institute of Contemporary Psychoanalysis, Los Angeles; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, UCLA School of Medicine.

Joel Pearl teaches philosophy at the Academic College of Communication, Rishon LeZion, Israel; he is also a psychoanalytic psychotherapist and has been a candidate member at the Tel-Aviv Institute for Contemporary Psychoanalysis. His research and publications, of which the volume under review is a prime example, have focused on phenomenology, psychoanalysis, and their intersection. A Question of Time: Freud in the Light of Heidegger's Temporality is a magnificent piece of interdisciplinary scholarship, one that wanned my own post-Cartesian psychoanalytic heart.

Like Binswanger (1946) and Boss (1963) before him, Pearl proceeds from the “top down”—that is, he begins with the philosophical presuppositions implicit in Freudian theory and with the fundamental existential structures delineated by Heidegger, and then goes on to show how the latter can (and should) transform psychoanalysis. Such an approach contrasts with one, such as my own (Stolorow 2011), that proceeds from the “bottom up”—that begins with clinical phenomena and an effort to rethink the psychoanalytic process phenomenologically and then finds philosophical support for this endeavor in Heidegger's existential analytic.

[[RunningHead]]

Moderators of Change in Psychoanalytic, Psychodynamic, and Cognitive-Behavioral Therapy

Dorothea Huber , Gerhard Henrich and Günther Klug

Günther Klug

Klinikum Harlaching
Sanitoriumplatz 2
81545 Munich
GERMANY
E-mail: d.huber@lrz.tu-muenchen.de

Günther Klug  

Outcome research in psychotherapy has proceeded remarkably, and that the effects of psychotherapy are statistically and clinically significant is empirically well founded. But “much more research needs to be conducted before the exact relationship between the process of the therapy and its outcome will be known” (Lambert and Ogles 2004, p. 181). For that reason, the focus of psychotherapy research has shifted from investigating outcome to a process-outcome approach. Following Laurenceau, Hayes, and Feldman (2007), process-outcome research addresses three sets of questions: For whom and under what conditions does change occur? (moderators of change); What is the shape of change? (trajectories of change); and Why and how is change occurring? (mediators of change).

The present study focuses on the examination of moderators of change. A moderator is “a characteristic that influences the direction or magnitude of the relationship between an independent and a dependent variable” (Kazdin 2007, p. 3). Moderators precede treatment and are not correlated with it (Kraemer et al. 2002). Identifying them helps in the prognosis of a course of therapy and in matching different patients to treatments. We included as putative moderators (1) treatment parameters (treatment modality, dose [session frequency x duration], therapeutic alliance), (2) sociodemographic variables (age, sex, partnership status), and (3) clinical patients' characteristics (diagnosis of personality disorder, emotional lability, and extraversion).

responseSet.title: Commentary on Chessick
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Commentary on Chessick

Richard Gottlieb

Richard Gottlieb

Richard Gottlieb  

That “psychoanalysis is an old man's game” may well be true, but in Richard Chessick's cautionary article he alerts us to vitally important but relatively unnoticed aspects of the game. He won't let us flinch or blink as with thirteen brief clinical vignettes he rivets our attention on the effects of how the analysand may perceive the senility1 of the analyst, and on the inevitable effects of those perceptions on the analytic process. At first this story seems long familiar from our literature, but it is not. While many before have commented on the need for the analyst to recognize and deal with the diminished and diminishing capacities that accompany aging, these useful observations have been viewed exclusively from only one side of the looking glass—the analyst's. But the analysand too is a participant-observer in the treatment, the one who sees the signs of the analyst's aging even if the analyst does not, cannot, or doesn't want to. Inevitably an analysand has reactions to these perceptions, reactions he too may deny or otherwise attempt to bury in what Chessick deems a dangerous “silent collusion” with the analyst that can “lead to destruction of the treatment” (p. 67).

It may be difficult or impossible for some analysands to share their unflatterng observations with their analysts as readily as Lewis Carroll's persona does in this famous interchange:

“You are old father William,” the young man said,

“And your hair has become very white;

And yet you incessantly stand on your head—

Do you think, at your age, it is right?”

Stephen Firestein (1994) warned of the usefulness of a professional last will and testament (cited in Goldberger 1994, p. 360 n). Many others have commented on the vulnerabilities, diminishing powers, and declining health of elderly analysts. This is a well-plowed field. By now we

1While the word senility is commonly used to designate the mental infirmities associated with old age, most dictionaries give at least equal weight to its meaning of simply “the state or quality of being old.

[[RunningHead]]

Naturalistic Outcomes of Evidence-Based Therapies for Borderline Personality Disorder at a University Clinic: A Quasi-Randomized Trial

Shilpa Sachdeva , Gregory Goldman , Georgian Mustata , Evan Deranja and Robert Gregory

Robert Gregory

713 Harrison Street
Syracuse, NY 13202
E-mail: sachdeva.shilpa@yahoo.com

Robert Gregory  

Both Dialectical Behavior Therapy (DBT) and Dynamic Deconstructive Psychotherapy (DDP) are listed in the National Registry of Evidence-Based Programs and Practices based on independent reviews of their

578

performance in randomized controlled trials for borderline personality disorder. However, little is known about their effectiveness in real-world settings.

DDP is a newer treatment, with demonstrated efficacy, but has been less extensively applied than DBT. In a twelve-month controlled trial, thirty subjects with Borderline Personality Disorder (BPD) and co-occurring active alcohol use disorders were randomized to either DDP or optimized community care. Almost half the subjects also met criteria for antisocial personality disorder (Gregory et al. 2008). Following twelve months of active treatment with DDP, they were then evaluated after an additional eighteen months of naturalistic care in the community. As applied in treating this low-functioning and highly comorbid BPD population, DDP demonstrated relatively good retention rates and large between-group treatment effects for core symptoms of BPD, depression, parasuicide behavior, alcohol misuse, recreational drug use, inpatient care, and perceived social support (Gregory, Delucia-Deranja, and Mogle 2010).

Subsequent research on the process mechanisms of this study indicated that adherence to DDP technique strongly correlated with positive outcome and that specific outcomes were associated with the application of specific techniques. These findings from psychotherapy process research strongly support the effectiveness of DDP techniques. After an independent review by the Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration, DDP was included on its National Registry of Evidence-Based Programs and Practices (www.nrepp.samhsa.gov).

DDP combines elements of translational neuroscience, object relations theory, and deconstructionist philosophy in a twelve-month treatment. Therapists provide weekly one-hour individual sessions helping patients activate those areas of the brain responsible for adaptive and integrative processing of emotionally charged experience. Sessions involve elaborating the sequence of recent episodes of interpersonal encounters and maladaptive behavior (evoking autobiographical memory), identifying and differentiating specific emotions associated with these episodes, and integrating different ways of making meaning of them. In addition, therapists provide novel experiences in the patient-therapist relationship that support individuation and challenge patients' basic assumptions about themselves and others. The treatment manual is available online at www.upstate.edu/ddp.

[[RunningHead]]

A Belated Book Review

Psychoanalysis and Its Application to Education. By Dimitrios Moraitis. Athens: Blazouthaki, 1928, 182 pp.

George Moraitis

George Moraitis

232 Pinehurst Drive
East Longmeadow, MA 01028-3062
E-mail: dr.georgemoraitis@gmail.com

George Moraitis   Faculty, Berkshire Psychoanalytic Institute; former Training and Supervising Analyst, Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis.

In this essay I will discuss the pioneering work of my father, Dimitrios Moraitis, who in 1928 published a psychoanalytic book in Athens under the title (rendered in English) Psychoanalysis and Its Application to Education.

Pioneering in intellectual fields is a complicated educational process of introducing new ways of thinking in an unfamiliar setting to an uninformed or misinformed audience. Pioneers are educators who often must move beyond the confines of their consultation room or classroom and pursue an unfamiliar path, usually without preparation or a set of instructions.

In the early stages of the psychoanalytic movement, psychoanalysts were all pioneers, Freud being of course the most important one. They joined the intellectual avant-garde of their time and, with great enthusiasm, zeal, and conviction, made concerted efforts to educate their audiences to the potential of psychoanalysis for facilitating psychological growth in those in treatment and in the culture more generally.

As a result of these efforts, psychoanalysis in Europe flourished for several years, until the Nazis destroyed everything. The diaspora that followed the exodus of psychoanalysts from Europe made them once again pioneers, now in their host countries. In the United States after the war, psychoanalysis experienced a meteoric rise that eventually brought this pioneering spirit to a halt. Instead of efforts to recruit new people to the profession, gatekeeping policies were established to exclude those perceived as unqualified.

Today the situation has changed again. It has now become abundantly clear that the “golden days” of psychoanalysis in the United States are over. Patients are hard to find, and so are candidates.

Expanding Borders

Minding the Markets: An Emotional Finance view of Financial Instability. By David Tuckett. Basingstoke, UK: Palgrave Macmillan, 2011, xx + 232 pp., $42.00.

Prudence Gourguechon

Prudence Gourguechon

1454 West Farragut Avenue #2
Chicago, IL 60640-2104
E-mail: prudygourguechon@gmail.com

Prudence Gourguechon   Faculty, Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis; Training and Supervising Analyst, Wisconsin Provisional Psychoanalytic Institute.

NPR's Renée Montagne was interviewing David Wessel, economics editor of the Wall Street Journal, on the morning of September 21, 2012. The Dow Jones was rising despite worldwide unrest, high unemployment, and the increasing likelihood that a presidential candidate thought to be unfriendly to Wall Street, Barack Obama, would win a second term in November. Montagne asked Wessel why, in the face of all this bad news, stocks were rising.

“Well,” Wessel replied, “it's always hard to know why the stock market does what it does. And it's important to remember that the mood on Wall Street could change abruptly at any moment.”

Wessel's language is so familiar it doesn't strike us as the bizarre cultural phenomenon it is. That's always how commentators describe Wall Street. Apparently, we have all known all along that the financial markets are utterly irrational, but I at least had not paused to consider the implications.

In Minding the Markets David Tuckett shines a laser-sharp light on the obvious but (for most of us) split-off fact that the financial markets are conducted according to the logic of the most primitive of emotions, with scarcely a dose of the rational choice that traditional economic theory assumes is running the show. Tuckett's book, subtitled An Emotional Finance View of Financial Instability, is a brilliant piece of applied psychoanalysis.

Writing for sophisticated nonpsychoanalysts—especially those in economics and finance—Tuckett's dense book takes an ambitious stab at explaining the implosion of the financial markets in 2008 using what might best be termed a “social-psychoanalytic” approach.

617

Tiziano Colibazzi

Tiziano Colibazzi

Tiziano Colibazzi  

334

Few would disagree with the assertion that emotions are an essential part of our clinical “navigation system.” Further, the way we conceptualize affects is central not only to psychoanalysis but also, one could argue, to all forms of psychotherapy. How exactly do we think of emotions during the clinical hour? If someone were to ask us, would we be able to articulate explicitly the theory we use during our sessions when we are trying to understand our patients' affective struggles? Do we treat emotions as irreducible biological quanta or as entities existing on a dimensional continuum? Do emotions come before or after physiological responses are set in motion? In other words, do we experience fear because our hearts are racing and our palms sweating, or do we end up with sweaty palms because we are fearful? These questions have occupied the minds of researchers since the seminal studies of William James, Carl Lange, Walter Cannon, Philip Bard, and, more recently, Antonio Damasio.

One of the main conceptual frameworks in affective research, the theory of basic emotions, holds that all emotional experiences are derived from a set of core emotions that are distinct and independent. According to this theory, anger and sadness, for instance, are thought to be produced in different neural structures and to be associated with discrete sets of behavioral, physiological, and psychological responses. Dimensional models of affect, by contrast, hypothesize that all emotions derive from a combination of psychological dimensions. One such model, the circumplex model of affect, holds that all emotional experiences result from a combination of two underlying dimensions, valence and arousal. While valence is the “hedonic tone” of the subjective experience (how pleasant or unpleasant an emotion is), arousal denotes the level of “internal energy.” In this theory, sadness is conceived as an emotion of negative valence characterized by low arousal, while fear is still an emotion of negative valence, but one characterized by a high level of arousal. The two underlying dimensions, valence and arousal, are interpreted by the individual so that emotional labels are assigned, allowing us to speak of “anger,” “joy,” “sadness,” and so forth. If ratings of arousal and valence for each type of emotion are plotted in a two-dimensional space, they tend to scatter in the space enclosed by a circle, forming a “circumplex.

As an aperitif to Freud's historic visit to Clark University in 1909, his American disciple A. A. Brill took him on a whirlwind tour of New York City. Among the sites they visited was Coney Island, which, according to Jones (1955, p. 56), Freud found fascinating. He described it as “a magnified Prater” (referring, of course, to the celebrated amusement park in Vienna).

Little else is known of his reaction to that visit; no reference to it appears in his published writings or in his known letters. But to a small group of Coney Island's mostly Jewish intellectuals that incident was never forgotten. In 1926, led by an artist and designer named Albert Grass, they founded the Coney Island Amateur Psychoanalytic Society. Drawing from the archives of the society, which she recently discovered, the artist Zoe Beloff (the editor of this intriguing book) writes that the group met monthly in an office on Surf Avenue, where they held lectures and discussions and, most important, screenings of filmed re-creations of their dreams, guided by their readings from “dog-eared copies of The Interpretation of Dreams” (Freud 1900; Beloff incorrectly gives the date as 1913, apparently using the publication date of Brill's translation).

[[RunningHead]]

Silence, Now

Jason Vega

Jason Vega

156 Fifth Avenue, Suite 1200
New York, NY 10010
E-mail: jasonwheelerphd@gmail.com

Jason Vega  

A panel discussion on the subject of silence may bring to mind the variously attributed maxim, “Talking about music is like dancing about architecture.” The implication is that the attempt to do either, the latter more clearly than the former, is what mid-century analytic philosophers called a category mistake: an illegitimate confusion of two radically different kinds of things. The artist and experimental musician Laurie Anderson (to whom the saying is sometimes credited) is reported to have supplied a riposte: “How about a square dance?” (Scott 2010). Psychoanalysts are ideally more comfortable than many with paradox, an attitude stemming from our acquaintance with the primary processes of the mind, to which appearance and contradiction are no obstacle. Hence, a panel of four distinguished analysts talking at length about silence should cause no more than a brief tremor of absurdity, easily shaken off. In the report to follow I will try to convey as much as possible of the proceedings and each member's contribution, though much detail must be omitted for the sake of brevity and confidentiality. In places I will add my own gloss or elaborations, and will note these as they occur. Otherwise, the reader should assume that the text is a paraphrase of the presentations.

The panel was structured by the chair, Melinda Gellman, to be more a roundtable discussion than the traditional sequence of speakers followed by a discussant offering a summary and interpretation of the presentations. After introductory remarks by Gellman, Jody Messier Davies and Ronald Britton presented their work, followed by a mid-panel discussion of their papers by all four panelists. Salman Akhtar and Virginia Ungar then presented their papers, followed again by discussion among the panelists. As reporter, I was then invited to comment before the floor was opened for questions.

Panel held at the Winter Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, January 18, 2013. Panelists: Melinda Gellman (chair), Jody Messier Davies, Ronald Britton, Salman Akhtar, Virginia Ungar.

Psychoanalysis and Empirical Research: The Example of Alexithymia

Graeme Taylor and R. Bagby

R. Bagby

104 Rosedale Heights Drive
Toronto, Ontario M4T 1C6
Canada
E-mail: graeme.taylor@utoronto.ca

R. Bagby   Professor of Psychology and Psychiatry, University of Toronto; Senior Scientist, Centre for Addiction and Mental Health, Toronto.

An extensive body of research on the alexithymia construct is reviewed to show how various empirical methodologies can be used to evaluate the validity and increase our understanding of theoretical and clinically derived psychoanalytic concepts. The historical background of alexithymia and the theoretical framework in which the construct was formulated are presented, after which measurement- and experiment-based approaches to construct validation are described. This is followed by a review of empirical investigations that have yielded evidence that alexithymia is a dimensional personality trait associated with several illnesses of interest to psychoanalysts. Empirical research also supports clinical observations and impressions that individuals with high degrees of alexithymia principally employ primitive defenses, have a limited capacity for empathy, exhibit deficits in mentalization, and do not respond well to traditional interpretive psychotherapies. Also reviewed is empirical research that implicates genetic and environmental/developmental factors in the etiology of alexithymia, in particular childhood trauma and insecure attachments, factors generally associated with deficits in affect development and affect regulation. The clinical relevance of the empirical research findings is discussed in the final section.

Shifting Sexual Cultures, the Potential Space of Online Relations, and the Promise of Psychoanalytic Listening

Ken Corbett

Ken Corbett

3 East 10th Street
New York, NY 10003
E-mail: kencorbett@kencorbett.com

Ken Corbett   Clinical Assistant Professor of Psychology, NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy.

How we listen to the potential space of online relations is rapidly becoming part of our daily work. Two treatments are examined where themes of sexual shame, repression, and relational difficulties were found and refound through online relating and hooking-up. Consideration is given to the ways in which online exploration opens a potential space that affords sexual practice and the reach of relationality. Moving with these patients into this potential space opened onto an intriguing and productive relational field. These cases help illuminate a shift in our modern social order, and how that shift has followed on a reconsideration of our psychoanalytic ideals about sexual well-being. We are now positioned to reflect not only on the evolution and revolution of our shifting sexual cultures, including the increasing role of technology in our modern relational world, but on how this new world finds its way into our consulting rooms.

responseSet.title: Core Issues in the Treatment of Personality-Disordered Patients
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Core Issues in the Treatment of Personality-Disordered Patients

Dan Buie

Dan Buie

38 Cliff Road
Wellesley, MA 02481
E-mail: vf-124@comcast.net

Dan Buie  

We all share in common our clinical experience with personality-disordered patients, and we note certain of their attributes. To a significant extent these patients are lost in terms of knowing who they are: they feel a dark aloneness; feel a grim sense of worthlessness; feel no warmth for themselves; and do not feel like a whole person with a genuine place with others. We could ascribe these attributes to their being deficient in five capacities basic to human stability, even viability. Because they are required for maintaining the self, I refer to them as self-maintenance capacities. They include (1) the capacity for self-realness, the experiential (not merely cognitive) knowledge that one's qualities, and the relationships between oneself and others, exist as real, that is, as actual; (2) the capacity for self-security, the ability to provide oneself an ongoing experience of being secure, which is the heritage of having developed with secure, warm holding; (3) the capacity for self-worth, the experiential knowledge of one's self-worth; (4) the capacity to experience love for oneself; and (5) the capacity to know one's identity experientially. To boil this down, people need to experience that they are real, secure from aloneness, worthwhile, and deserving of their love, and that they possess an identity.

The major difference between neurotic and personality-disordered patients is that neurotic patients have the capacities to provide themselves these basic-self experiences. Personality-disordered patients do not. The aim of treatment with the latter, then, is to help them develop these capacities. Though I have listed these self-maintenance capacities as if they occur as distinct phenomena, in the clinical situation they do not present so neatly. They overlap and intertwine. I have listed them separately simply to help us think about them.

Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute. Plenary address, American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, January 2012. Submitted for publication August 13, 2012.

responseSet.title: How to Help Get Stuck Analyses Unstuck
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

How to Help Get Stuck Analyses Unstuck

Rebecca Chaplan

Rebecca Chaplan

40 West 77th Street, 1F
New York, NY 10024
E-mail: rc361@columbia.edu

Rebecca Chaplan  

This panel, attractively but somewhat misleadingly titled, presented three cases in which the analyst had despaired at the lack of movement in the case, at the patient's seeming imperviousness to analytic understanding of the enacted fantasy, and had all but lost hope. In all three cases the “stuckness” was understood as a shared dynamic between analyst and patient, with the analyst experiencing deep immersion in a role unconsciously assigned by the patient. The analyst's ability to use this countertransference response and think about what was being enacted became a way into understanding the forces resisting change. We were treated to three evocative case histories in which the analyst found a solution, from within the patient's worldview, to resist the gravitational attraction of the stuck pattern, with each solution specific to the case but based on some shared principles. Thus, rather than offers of how-to advice, what emerged in a morning of generative interaction and little controversy were ideas about what the three cases had in common and what had helped.

Rosemary Balsam, as chair, introduced the panelists. She began by noting that what we refer to colloquially as “stuckness” is a clinical entity that overlaps with concepts such as “impasse,” “negative therapeutic reaction,” “enactment,” “repetition compulsion,” “countertransference,” and “transference” in its broadest sense. She added that Stanley Coen, who first suggested the panel, had chosen a “pragmatic” word “that we can all relate to in an immediate way, and the details of what any one of us considers a ‘stuck’ development in the analytic situation will be both overdetermined and multidetermined.”

Balsam referred to contemporary thinking on the topic, including the long-running APsaA discussion group run by Steven Goldberg and Judy

Panel held at the Spring Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Chicago, June 17, 2012. Panelists: Rosemary Balsam (chair), Stanley Coen, Frances Arnold, Dionne Powell.

responseSet.title: Analysis Begins in the Analyst's Mind: Conceptual and Technical Considerations on Recommending Analysis
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Analysis Begins in the Analyst's Mind: Conceptual and Technical Considerations on Recommending Analysis

Lena Ehrlich

Lena Ehrlich

555 East William Street, Suite 22C
Ann Arbor, MI 48104
E-mail: lenaehrlich@comcast.net

Lena Ehrlich  

For certain patients who approach analysts for treatment, analysis remains the only treatment that can provide the urgent and at times lifesaving help they need. At the same time, recommending analysis presents analysts with a surprisingly challenging emotional task. Because patients will not be able to get analytic help unless the analyst recommends it and facilitates the patient's engagement, it is vital that analysts identify the conditions that make the beginning of analysis possible. Analysis, it is argued, begins in the analyst's mind: how analysts think about their function, their patients, and the analytic process determines in great measure whether analysis will begin. Six essential components of the analyst's mindset are presented, as well as technical considerations about recommending analysis that are based on this mindset and that have been useful in initiating analysis. A detailed clinical example is provided to illustrate how the analyst's thinking informed the initial phase of a treatment with a patient who engaged in a productive analysis.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Special Problems for the Elderly Psychoanalyst in the Psychoanalytic Process

Richard Chessick

Richard Chessick

S9400 Drake Avenue
Evanston, IL 60203-1106
E-mail: r-chessick@northwestern.edu

Richard Chessick   Emeritus Professor of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences, Northwestern University; Emeritus Training and Supervising Analyst, Center for Psychoanalytic Study, Chicago; Emeritus Senior Attending Psychiatrist, Evanston Hospital.

The psychoanalytic process takes on a special ambience when the analyst is clearly elderly. The effects of this ambience on the the aging analyst's patients are discussed, and the sparse literature on the subject is reviewed. Clinical vignettes illustrate a number of these effects on the analytic process. Dealing with these special problems requires not only the analyst's awareness of their existence but a continual monitoring of the transference-countertransference in order to avoid a silent collusion of patient and analyst to pretend these problems do not exist. The dangerous consequences of being unaware of the situation, for both patient and analyst, are discussed. If the influence of the patient's perception of the analyst's aging is ignored, it may lead to destruction of the treatment either through massive acting out or by a hopeless stalemate with or without the development of an endless psychoanalysis.

responseSet.title: Treatment Notes: Objective Measures of Language Style Point to Clinical Insights
responseSet.authorMast: Leon Hoffman,  Jane Algus,  Will Braun,  Wilma Bucci and  Bernard Maskit
responseSet.authorCitation: Hoffman, L., Algus, J., Braun, W., Bucci, W. & Maskit, B.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Treatment Notes: Objective Measures of Language Style Point to Clinical Insights

Leon Hoffman , Jane Algus , Will Braun , Wilma Bucci and Bernard Maskit

Bernard Maskit

167 East 67th Street
New York, NY 10065
E-mail: hoffman.leon@gmail.com

Bernard Maskit  

Application of a computerized text analysis procedure is proposed that has the potential for use by psychoanalytic and psychodynamic clinicians: the systematic examination of linguistic style as reflected by clinicians in their ongoing process and case notes, which are ubiquitous in the mental health field. The studies reported here are, as far as is known, the first attempts to study treatment notes systematically using such procedures. Linguistic measures are used to track the trajectory of the clinical process throughout the treatment in two contrasting cases, one rated successful, the other not. The computerized linguistic analysis used here focuses on two analytically relevant linguistic variables: Mean High Referential Activity (MHW), a measure of the degree to which language is connected to emotional processing, and Reflection (REF), the use of words referring to logical functions. Changes in the relative position of these measures indicate nodal points in the treatment that might be analytically or therapeutically problematic, and that might be overlooked in a solely clinical reading. The analyst's activity as reported in notes during such nodal periods is clinically examined to see how it may have affected the course of the analysis. This method has the potential for use in ongoing treatments, and may help clinicians refine their interventions.

responseSet.authorMast: Barbara F. Marcus and  Susan McNamara
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“Strange and Otherwise Unaccountable Actions”: Category, Conundrum, and Trans Identities

Barbara Marcus and Susan McNamara

Susan McNamara

11 South Main Street, Suite 2
Middletown, CT 06457
E-mail: sxmcnam@mac.com

Susan McNamara   Private practice.

Despite recognizing that gender identity is a complex compromise formation, psychotherapists struggle to tolerate gender variance. We still tend to favor binary gender identities and clear developmental lines, rather than embracing a stance of subversive curiosity about the variability and fluidity of gender in our patients. When gender identity is fluid or ambiguous, countertransferential affective disturbances can arise that meld states of abjection and excitement, challenging theoretical constructs and threatening therapeutic neutrality. Case material from the treatment of a female-to-male trans person is presented from the perspective of the transference-countertransference matrix.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis as Poetry
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis as Poetry

Jeanine Vivona

Jeanine Vivona

11 Bala Avenue
Bala Cynwyd, PA 19004
E-mail: jvivona@tcnj.edu

Jeanine Vivona  

Like psychoanalysis, poetry is possible because of the nature of verbal language, particularly its potentials to evoke the sensations of lived experience. These potentials are vestiges of the personal relational context in which language is learned, without which there would be no poetry and no psychoanalysis. Such a view of language infuses psychoanalytic writings on poetry, yet has not been fully elaborated. To further that elaboration, a poem by Billy Collins is presented to illustrate the sensorial and imagistic potentials of words, after which the interpersonal processes of language development are explored in an attempt to elucidate the original nature of words as imbued with personal meaning, embodied resonance, and emotion. This view of language and the verbal form allows a fuller understanding of the therapeutic processes of speech and conversation at the heart of psychoanalysis, including the relational potentials of speech between present individuals, which are beyond the reach of poetry. In one sense, the work of the analyst is to create language that mobilizes the experiential, memorial, and relational potentials of words, and in so doing to make a poet out of the patient so that she too can create such language.

responseSet.title: Death, Time, and Psychosis
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Death, Time, and Psychosis

Riccardo Lombardi

Riccardo Lombardi

Via dei Fienaroli 36
Rome 00153
ITALY
E-mail: dr.riccardolombardi@libero.it

Riccardo Lombardi  

Working through the awareness of death and the consciousness of time, it is hypothesized, plays a decisive role in the analytic process and in the mental growth of psychotic analysands, as well as in the integration of the psychotic areas in healthier patients. Clinical material is presented from a psychotic woman treated analytically, four sessions a week, for twelve years. The patient suffered several acute relapses, during which the analytic work was not interrupted. Her fourth psychotic episode in the course of analysis, which involved a delusion about gray men and the theft of time, is explored in particular depth. This phase fostered the patient's recognition of the value of time, together with the acquisition of her own center of psychosensory integration, the basis of an ability to learn from experience.

responseSet.title: Relational Freedom and Therapeutic Action
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Relational Freedom and Therapeutic Action

Donnel Stern

Donnel Stern

24 East 82nd Street
New York, NY 10028
E-mail: donnelstern@gmail.com

Donnel Stern  

Therapeutic action depends on our freedom to allow ourselves novel, unbidden experience. How does this novelty arise? What is the process by which some portion of the possibilities inherent in any moment's unformulated experience are created or selected and emerge in consciousness? And what does it mean to think of freedom in this context? What does it mean for the formulation of experience to be free? In the frame of reference adopted here, the formulation of experience depends on the conscious and unconscious events of the interpersonal field. The field facilitates some formulations of experience and prevents others. Thus, whatever we can do to make it possible for the analytic relationship to evolve freely, without constraint or constriction, is the best way we have to encourage the freedom to experience. “Relational freedom” underpins therapeutic action. A clinical case is described at length to illustrate these ideas.

responseSet.title: Enactment and the Emergence of New Relational Organization
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Enactment and the Emergence of New Relational Organization

Boston Change Process Study Group

Boston Change Process Study Group

36 Birch Hill Road
West Newton, MA 02465-2552
E-mail: jpnahum@massmed.org

Boston Change Process Study Group  

Enactments are investigated from the process-oriented focus of our therapeutic approach. By embedding their occurrence within the ongoing flow of nonlinear dyadic process, we focus on the subtle back-and-forth between patient and analyst, as well as the importance of what we call now moments. An alternative to the dissociative self-state model is offered that emphasizes implicit memory processes in bodily comportment and style of relating with others. We suggest that change occurs through the emergence of new relational (i.e., procedural) skills within a therapeutic relationship that is self-organizing at more inclusive levels. Treating enactment as an emergent property of the dyad means not concentrating on the level of the individual components of a system. Rather, it means regarding enactment as a property of the entire system, without which there would be no emergent property. Going forward, we suggest use of the term relational apprehension in referring to the complex process of grasping a gestalt of relational meaning as an integration of perceptions, feelings, images and imaginings, sensations, fantasies, thoughts, and intuitions. Two brief case vignettes from the literature are discussed in order to illustrate this view.

responseSet.title: Matisse's “Radical Invention”: A Window on Therapeutic Change
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Matisse's “Radical Invention”: A Window on Therapeutic Change

J. David Miller

J. David Miller

3 Washington Circle NW, Suite 405
Washington, DC 20037
E-mail: jdmiller1957@earthlink.net

J. David Miller  

A recent exhibition at the Museum of Modern Art, “Matisse: Radical Invention 1913-1917,” focused on a breakthrough in Matisse's art that the curators attribute to the impact of World War I. That this dramatic change may be linked to the trauma of war is explored by studying the art itself, biographical material, and the artist's own insightful comments. The thesis is advanced that the catastrophe in Europe aggravated Matisse's neurotic conflicts and that his art reflects his effort to resolve these conflicts through new compromise formations and, more significantly, sublimation. Sublimation is discussed as it relates not only to the “radical invention” of Matisse's wartime art but also to a concurrent transformation of his personality. These ideas about Matisse are applied to clinical vignettes in a consideration of the role of sublimation in a contemporary view of therapeutic change.

China on the Mind. By Christopher Bollas. New York: Routledge, 2013, x + 158 pp., $110.00 hardcover, $26.95 paperback.

David Scharff

David Scharff

6612 Kennedy Drive
Chevy Chase, MD 20815-6504
E-mail: davidscharff@theipi.org

David Scharff   Teaching Analyst, Washington Center for Psychoanalysis; Supervising Analyst, International Institute for Psychoanalytic Training; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Georgetown University.

Christopher Bollas's China on the Mind caught my eye because I have an interest in the application of analytic therapies in mainland China and, with much less experience, Taiwan. I have been teaching object relations psychoanalysis in China since 2007 and, most recently, psychoanalytic couple therapy in Beijing to students from all over China. I have become interested in the problem of how our Chinese colleagues experience psychoanalytic ideas, and especially how they put them into practice. There are practical issues involving the prior lack of training, the fact that psychotherapy and psychoanalysis are foreign imports with no history in China before 1929, eradication of what little had begun between 1949 and 1976, and new importation beginning in the 1980s. Moreover, the Chinese Communist Party had launched an all-out war on everything Western from 1949 on, while also eradicating, with seeming success, the philosophical underpinnings of traditional Chinese culture. Because Confucianism, Daoism, and Buddhism all but disappeared from sight in those years, it was assumed by many that their contribution to the structure of Chinese morality and psychological structure had also disappeared.

However, many of us have found that this appearance is deceptive. Mao was not so different from many Chinese emperors of the last twenty-five hundred years. He was both more successful and more destructive than many, and he did transform many Confucian deep assumptions, but fundamentally he built on them to fashion the well-known cult of his personality that bound the Chinese people of the Communist era to him. He used Confucian morality, which begins with loyalty to the emperor, to his advantage, and he elevated the idea of loyalty to the group to the next highest principle. Loyalty to the party expressed as fidelity to the “people” came now before loyalty to the family and the father, but the criterion of correct thought was fealty to Mao and his ideas of the moment. It was distorted Confucianism, but Confucian nonetheless.

responseSet.title: “Action” in Psychoanalysis: A Comparison between Adult and Adolescent Analysis
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“Action” in Psychoanalysis: A Comparison between Adult and Adolescent Analysis

Edward Kenny

Edward Kenny

Edward Kenny  

In Plato's allegory of the cave, human cave-dwellers perceive only shadows of actors and action occurring beyond the threshold of the cave. Barred from direct observation, they nonetheless glean an idea of the outside forms. Plato analogized this situation to our limitations in comprehending the eternal forms that he said embodied truth and beauty. This report is itself a kind of shadow play, a paradoxical effort to both capture and obscure the forms and movements of the panel on action held at the 2012 Winter Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association. The unobstructed focus of the panel was the graphic process material of consecutive analytic sessions from treatments conducted by David Ott and Ruth Graver. Just one step shy of full-color video, the vivid treatment dialogues were framed and theorized by Karen Gilmore, Eslee Samberg, and Mark Smaller. By design, Ott and Graver did not disclose their personal reactions or the overall narrative arc of the treatments; rather, they performed dramatic moments of the action, akin to a movie preview. With regret, I must withhold certain details of their rich process material to guard the privacy of the analysands, particularly in the case presented by Graver. Nevertheless, I will attempt to trace a fine line between complete obscurity and too telling revelation in an effort to sketch the action for the reader. In addition, I will include comments from my own perspective—influenced by the work of Kernberg and other object relations theorists—along with suggestions for future consideration.

In her succinct yet comprehensive framing remarks, Karen Gilmore outlined the rationale for showing just the raw work and focusing on action as the theme for the panel. “At least two streams of psychoanalytic

Panel held at the Winter Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, January 14, 2012. Panelists: Karen Gilmore (chair), Ruth Graver, David Ott, Eslee Samberg, Mark Smaller.

The author thanks Michael Beldoch and David Ott for their help in the preparation of this report.

responseSet.title: Varieties of Psychoanalytic Experience: Lessons from Returning Patients
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Varieties of Psychoanalytic Experience: Lessons from Returning Patients

Richard Almond

Richard Almond

1520 University Avenue
Palo Alto, CA 94301-2031
E-mail: rjalmond@stanford.edu

Richard Almond  

Psychoanalytic discourse often describes an ideal course of analysis, one that then becomes a norm for analysts and analytic discussion. It is well to remind ourselves that this ideal, or norm, developed first when Freud was conducting analyses in six months or a year, and believed that familiarity with unconscious process would be curative. Our idea that an analysis has a predictable course, a completeness in addressing conflict, and a decisive termination stems from these early days. Contemporary discussions of crises and impasses in treatment, along with recognition of enactments and countertransference in general, have enriched our picture, just as Freud did in “Analysis Terminable and Interminable” (1937). Investigations of the actual course of patients in and following analysis through follow-up has added further to creating a real understanding of where analytic experience may fit into patients' lives (Schachter 2009; Wallerstein 1986).

A recent invitation to report on my experience with psychoanalytic patients who returned for more treatment later on, and the lessons I learned from them, led me to reflect on my forty years of clinical work, as well as on cases I have encountered in reading, teaching, and supervising, and in study groups and public presentations. The result has been a discovery of the wide variety of analytic experiences. I was surprised to realize how many of my analytic patients had in fact returned. As I tried to generalize from these experiences, I noticed that there was an immense variation. People came back for a wide variety of reasons, and their postanalytic contact took a wide variety of forms. As I tried to generalize, my broadest impression was the most obvious one—that analysis is a profound experience, one that in almost all my returning patients enabled them to think and feel about themselves differently.

Nancy Olson

Nancy Olson

47 Trumbull Street
New Haven, CT 06510-1004

Nancy Olson   Assistant Clinical Professor of Psychiatry and Coordinator, Muriel Gardiner Program for Psychoanalysis and the Humanities, Yale University; graduate, Western New England Institute for Psychoanalysis.

My introduction to Marion Milner began when I was a graduate student in art history. While browsing one day, I came across a slim paperback, On Not Being Able to Paint. The title was catchy, so too the cover, in what might be called shocking pink. I didn't love the color, wasn't trying to paint at the time, and wasn't sure what sort of book this might be. Perhaps for these reasons it came home with me, not to be read until later when I became a candidate in analytic training. When at last I read On Not Being Able to Paint, I felt befriended. I searched for Milner's other books, then mostly out of print.

Jonathan Lear's A Case for Irony

A Case for Irony. By Jonathan Lear. Commentary by Cora Diamond, Christine M. Korsgaard, Richard Moran, and Robert A. Paul. Cambridge: Harvard University Press, 2011, xiv + 210 pp., $29.95.

Bennett Simon

Bennett Simon

44B Burroughs Street
Jamaica Plain, MA 02130
E-mail: bennett_simon@hms.harvard.edu

Bennett Simon   Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School.

For Jonathan Lear, trained in two dialogical fields, philosophy and psychoanalysis, The Case for Irony is another expression of his long-standing commitment to understanding the relationship between the two. Exploring that relationship goes back to the early years of psychoanalysis—the minutes of the Vienna Society record several philosophers participating in those meetings—and Lear has a number of predecessors and colleagues in this enterprise.1 His own labors span now some twenty years of publications. Among the themes running through much of his work are two that are most salient in this book: one is the question of transformation: How do philosophy and psychoanalysis each aspire to bring about profound change in mind and heart, including transformations in the social and political sphere? The second is how psychoanalysis and contemporary moral philosophy interdigitate and, especially in this book, how moral philosophy needs psychoanalysis for a richer and more realistic view of how the person as moral agent is constituted. His books, essays, and collections of essays, while always interdisciplinary in their larger purpose, have varied in the degree to which they are addressed primarily to psychoanalysts, philosophers, or more general audiences (e.g., earlier essays in The New Republic). The present book is heavily tilted toward a readership of philosophers, dealing in great depth, it seems to me, with issues of current concern in contemporary philosophy dealing with morals and ethics.

1A partial list of those in the United States and Great Britain includes Marcia Cavell, Stanley Cavell, Donald Davidson, Adolf Grünbaum, Charles Hanly, Jerome Neu, Jennifer Radden, John Wisdom, J. O. Wisdom, and Richard Wollheim. They often write from very different perspectives, and have not always systematically referred to one another.147
